tasks.title: Tasks
tasks.description: The clearest signal of a productive day: you finished what you said you would. Break it down, start small, check it off.
tasks.emptyBody: Start with one thing that would make today feel like a win. Break it into steps if it feels big — the first step is enough.
tasks.emptyTodayBody: A clean slate. Add one task you want done today — even one small win changes your momentum.
tasks.upcomingEmptyBody: No tasks scheduled. Add one to see your week at a glance.
tasks.clockNote: Tasks are scheduled in your local timezone
tasks.plannedDateHelper: Scheduling a task makes you 2–3× more likely to do it. Even "today" is enough — specificity is the point.
tasks.subtasksHelper: Break it into micro-steps. Research shows task initiation — not execution — is the hardest part for most people. Starting the first step is 80% of the work.
tasks.focusHelper: The Pomodoro technique (25-min focused sprints) matches your brain's natural 90-min ultradian cycle. Even one session a day compounds significantly over a month.
tasks.timerDone: Focus session complete. You just did something most people don't: you protected your attention. That compounds.
tasks.intentionPlaceholder: When I finish breakfast, I will…
tasks.intentionHelper: Implementation intentions (when/where) double follow-through rates — proven across 94 studies. Your brain treats this as a pre-commitment.
tasks.timerActiveTaskHint: Timer running — see task card above for controls.
tasks.timerStartHint: ▶ Tap "🍅 {{minutes}} min" on any task above to start a focused Pomodoro session.

cleanSpace.title: Clean your immediate space
cleanSpace.why: Peterson: order in your environment reduces cognitive load and signals to your brain that you can handle responsibility.
cleanSpace.step1: Clear the surface you work at
cleanSpace.step2: Remove trash or clutter from the room
cleanSpace.step3: Put one thing away that's been out of place

digitalDeclutter.title: Digital declutter
digitalDeclutter.why: Newport: attention is finite. Every app and notification competes for cognitive bandwidth. Remove what doesn't serve your goals.
digitalDeclutter.step1: Delete apps you haven't opened in 30 days
digitalDeclutter.step3: Clear your phone's home screen to only essentials

designEnv.title: Design your environment for better habits
designEnv.why: Clear: environment design is the invisible hand of behavior. Make the right choice the easy choice.
designEnv.step1: Identify one bad habit and remove its cue from your space
designEnv.step2: Make one good habit easier (place the item in plain sight)
designEnv.step3: Set up your work area before you finish today

deepWork.title: Deep work block
deepWork.why: Newport: the ability to focus without distraction is becoming increasingly rare and increasingly valuable. One focused session beats four scattered ones.
deepWork.step1: Choose ONE important task — the one you've been avoiding
deepWork.step2: Set a 90-minute timer with all notifications off
deepWork.step3: Write down your goal for this block before starting
deepWork.step4: Review what you produced at the end

shutdownRitual.title: Daily shutdown ritual
shutdownRitual.why: Newport: without a clear ending to your workday, your brain keeps cycling through unfinished work. A shutdown ritual creates genuine rest.
shutdownRitual.step1: Scan your task list — capture anything uncaptured

brainDump.title: Brain dump
brainDump.why: Allen: your brain is for having ideas, not storing them. Every uncaptured thought costs attention. Empty it completely.
brainDump.step2: Write every open loop, worry, or to-do in your head — no filtering
brainDump.step3: Review the list and mark which items actually need action
brainDump.step4: Pick the single most important one and schedule it

weeklyReview.title: Weekly review
weeklyReview.why: Allen: the weekly review is the master key to GTD. Without it, your system decays. With it, you stay clear and in control.
weeklyReview.step1: Clear your inbox and capture any new commitments
weeklyReview.step2: Review your projects list — is anything stuck?
weeklyReview.step3: Check what you accomplished this week

nextAction.title: Define the next action
nextAction.why: Allen: vague projects don't move. The moment you define a physical next action, procrastination drops and momentum returns.
nextAction.step1: Pick your most stuck or procrastinated project
nextAction.step2: Ask: what is the very next physical action required?
nextAction.step3: Add that specific action to your task list and schedule it

morningSunlight.title: Morning sunlight
morningSunlight.why: Huberman: morning light exposure sets your circadian clock, boosts cortisol at the right time, and improves mood, focus, and sleep quality.
morningSunlight.step1: Go outside within 30–60 minutes of waking
morningSunlight.step2: Stay outside for 10–15 minutes without sunglasses
morningSunlight.step3: Walk or stand — movement helps absorption

moveBody.title: Move your body
moveBody.why: Huberman: 20–30 minutes of cardio raises BDNF, dopamine, and serotonin — the trifecta for mood, learning, and stress resilience.
moveBody.step1: Pick one form of movement (walk, run, lift, swim)
moveBody.step3: Do it before checking news or social media if possible

fixSleep.title: Fix your sleep schedule
fixSleep.why: Breus: sleep is the single highest-leverage intervention for mental performance, emotional regulation, and physical health.
fixSleep.step1: Choose a consistent wake time and stick to it for 2 weeks
fixSleep.step2: Set an alarm to start winding down 1 hour before bed
fixSleep.step3: Remove your phone from the bedroom or put it across the room

reduceAlcohol.title: Reduce alcohol this week
reduceAlcohol.why: Huberman: even moderate alcohol disrupts REM sleep, suppresses neurogenesis, and lowers serotonin baselines the following day.
reduceAlcohol.step1: Identify which days/situations you drink most
reduceAlcohol.step2: Set a specific rule (e.g., only weekends, only 1 drink)
reduceAlcohol.step3: Replace the ritual with a non-alcoholic alternative for one week

journal.title: Write one page
journal.why: Peterson: writing clarifies thought. When you articulate your fears and intentions, you stop carrying them as a vague weight and start engaging with them directly.
journal.step1: Write one page — stream of consciousness, no editing
journal.step2: End with: what is one thing I could do differently tomorrow?

gratitude.title: Gratitude practice
gratitude.why: Emmons: specific gratitude — naming what and why — measurably increases wellbeing, reduces envy, and strengthens relationships over time.
gratitude.step1: Write 3 specific things you're grateful for (not generic)
gratitude.step2: For each one, write WHY you're grateful for it
gratitude.step3: Text one of them to the person involved if applicable

comparePastSelf.title: Compare yourself to who you were yesterday
comparePastSelf.why: Peterson: comparing yourself to others breeds resentment. Comparing yourself to your past self breeds improvement and self-respect.
comparePastSelf.step1: Write 3 ways you've improved in the past 6 months
comparePastSelf.step2: Identify one area where you're still struggling
comparePastSelf.step3: Set one small, specific improvement goal for this week only

tellTruth.title: Tell the truth — or at least don't lie
tellTruth.why: Peterson: small lies compound into a distorted life. Each honest act strengthens your sense of self and reduces the anxiety of maintaining a false front.
tellTruth.step1: Identify one thing you've been avoiding saying or doing
tellTruth.step2: Write what is actually true about your situation
tellTruth.step3: Decide on one honest action you can take this week

takeResponsibility.title: Take responsibility for one thing
takeResponsibility.why: Peterson: before you can change anything outside, you have to take ownership of what's inside your control. This is where real agency begins.
takeResponsibility.step1: Name one problem in your life you've been blaming on others or circumstances
takeResponsibility.step2: Write what part of this is actually within your control
takeResponsibility.step3: Choose one action to take that is fully in your power

reachOut.title: Reach out to someone
reachOut.why: Dunbar: humans evolved for a social group of ~150. Regular contact with your inner circle is not optional — isolation increases mortality more than smoking.
reachOut.step1: Think of one person you haven't spoken to in over 2 weeks
reachOut.step2: Send a genuine, non-generic message (not just 'hey')
reachOut.step3: Ask one real question about their life

repairRelationship.title: Repair a relationship
repairRelationship.why: Gottman: repair attempts — even small ones — predict relationship survival more than conflict frequency does. Avoidance always makes it worse.
repairRelationship.step1: Identify the relationship you've been avoiding or neglecting
repairRelationship.step2: Write what you'd want to say — unsent letter first
repairRelationship.step3: Reach out and say one true thing without defensiveness

expressAppreciation.title: Express appreciation
expressAppreciation.why: Gottman: the 5:1 ratio — five positive interactions for every negative one — determines relationship health. Deliberate appreciation is how you build it.
expressAppreciation.step1: Name one person who deserves acknowledgment today
expressAppreciation.step2: Tell them something specific (not general) that you appreciate
expressAppreciation.step3: Do it in person or voice — not text if possible

deliberatePractice.title: Deliberate practice session
deliberatePractice.why: Newport: skill accumulation through deliberate practice is how you build career capital — the rare and valuable abilities that give you control over your work life.
deliberatePractice.step1: Identify the single most valuable skill in your field
deliberatePractice.step2: Find the edge of your current ability in that skill
deliberatePractice.step3: Practice just beyond your comfort zone for 45–60 minutes
deliberatePractice.step4: Review what was hard and what improved

identifyMission.title: Identify your career mission
identifyMission.why: Newport: a compelling career mission emerges from the intersection of your career capital and the adjacent possible — you can't shortcut it, but you can map it.
identifyMission.step1: Write what you're genuinely good at (skills others pay for)
identifyMission.step2: Write what problems in the world you care about
identifyMission.step3: Find one intersection between those two lists
identifyMission.step4: Research one person doing work at that intersection

sayNo.title: Say no to one commitment
sayNo.why: Newport: every yes is a no to deep work. Protecting your time requires deliberate subtraction, not just better scheduling.
sayNo.step1: List your current commitments — every yes you've given
sayNo.step2: Identify one that is not aligned with your priorities
sayNo.step3: Decline or exit it with a clear, honest response

listDebts.title: List all your debts
listDebts.why: Hammer: you cannot fight what you refuse to see. Most people don't know their actual numbers. Writing them down is the first act of financial courage.
listDebts.step1: Open every account statement you've been avoiding
listDebts.step2: Write each debt: creditor, balance, interest rate, minimum payment
listDebts.step3: Sort by interest rate (highest first = avalanche method)
listDebts.step4: Total the debt — write down the actual number

emergencyFund.title: Start an emergency fund
emergencyFund.why: Hammer: without a cash buffer, every unexpected expense becomes debt. Even $500 saved breaks the cycle of reactive borrowing.
emergencyFund.step1: Calculate your monthly essential expenses (rent + food + bills)
emergencyFund.step2: Identify how much you currently have in savings
emergencyFund.step3: Set up a separate high-yield savings account if you haven't
emergencyFund.step4: Automate a fixed amount to transfer on payday — even $50 counts

trackSpending.title: Track spending for one week
trackSpending.why: Hammer: most people guess at their spending and are wrong by 30–50%. One week of real data reveals the leaks that no budget plan can fix in the abstract.
trackSpending.step1: Open your bank/card statements for the last 7 days
trackSpending.step2: Categorize every transaction: need, want, or save
trackSpending.step4: Find the one 'want' category that surprised you most

cancelSubs.title: Cancel unused subscriptions
cancelSubs.why: Hammer: subscription creep is the silent killer of household budgets. Most people pay for 2–3 services they forgot they had.
cancelSubs.step3: Cancel any you haven't used in 30+ days

negotiateBill.title: Negotiate one bill
negotiateBill.why: Hammer: a single 15-minute phone call can save $10–$50/month. Frugality is not deprivation — it's refusing to pay more than necessary.
negotiateBill.step1: Choose one recurring bill (internet, phone, insurance)
negotiateBill.step2: Research what competitors charge for the same service
negotiateBill.step3: Call and say you're considering switching — ask for a better rate
negotiateBill.step4: Document the outcome and time saved vs money saved

validation.durationPositive: Duration must be greater than zero.
validation.invalidDateTime: Enter a valid date and time.
validation.pointsPositive: Points must be greater than zero.
validation.subtasksRange: Enter between {{min}} and {{max}} steps, or leave blank.
validation.endAfterStart: End time must be after the start time.
validation.nonNegativeNumber: Enter a zero or positive number.
validation.nonNegativeInteger: Enter a whole number that is zero or greater.

settingsGamificationHelper: Opt in to XP, streaks, and cosmetic unlocks. You can disable this any time.
settingsAnimationHelper: Disable non-essential animations to keep the interface calm.

budget.title: Budget
budget.placeholder: Budget tools will track income and expenses, categorize them and simulate debt payoff.
budget.debtSnowball: Debt snowball coaching and payoff projections will appear here alongside your budget overview.
budget.remainingHint: Zero-based budgeting: give every dollar a job before the month starts. When this hits 0, every cent is intentional — that's the whole point.
budget.planActualHint: Top bar shows planned, bottom bar shows actual.
budget.payoffEmpty: Add debts and minimum payments to simulate payoff.
budget.reviewHelper: A 5-minute weekly budget check-in — even just glancing at the numbers — is what separates people who stay on track from those who drift. Log it.
budget.reviewDisabled: Enable gamification to track your weekly budget streak.
budget.zeroBasedExplain: Zero-based budgeting: give every dollar a job. When Remaining hits zero, every cent is intentional — that's the whole point.
budget.remainingExplain: The gap between what you earn and what you've allocated. Your goal: reach zero before the month ends — not by spending more, but by planning more.
budget.needTooltip: Essentials you can't function without: rent, groceries, transport, utilities, medication. If life stops without it, it's a Need.
budget.wantTooltip: Things that improve your life but aren't critical: dining out, streaming, hobbies, clothes beyond basics. Not 'bad money' — just honest about what they are.
budget.goalTooltip: Money working toward your future self: emergency fund, savings, investments, vacation. Pay yourself first — this category builds the life you want.
budget.debtTooltip: Existing obligations with interest costs. Tracked separately so you can see the real total cost and build an exit plan.
budget.snowballExplain: Pay minimums on everything, throw every extra dollar at the smallest balance. Each debt cleared is a concrete win — and wins keep you going.
budget.avalancheExplain: Pay minimums on everything, attack the highest interest rate first. Mathematically optimal — saves the most money over time.
budget.payoffInfeasible: Current payments are too low to reduce this debt. Increase minimum or extra payment.
budget.chartAriaLabel: Planned vs actual spending by category

diagnostic.title: Financial Diagnostic
diagnostic.subtitle: Enter your monthly numbers to get an honest picture of your finances.
diagnostic.fixedSection: Fixed costs — every month, same amount
diagnostic.variableSection: Variable costs — changes month to month
diagnostic.prefillTitle: Pre-fill your budget with these numbers
diagnostic.prefillSubtitle: Your expense categories will be added to your budget automatically.
diagnostic.alertHousingCritical: Housing is {{pct}}% of income — critical. Aim for under 30%.
diagnostic.alertHousingWarning: Housing is {{pct}}% of income. The target is under 30%.
diagnostic.alertHousingGood: Housing is {{pct}}% of income. Well within range.
diagnostic.alertDeficit: You are spending more than you earn — deficit of {{symbol}}{{amount}}/mo.
diagnostic.alertDiningOut: Dining out is {{pct}}% of income. This is a high-leverage area.
diagnostic.alertSubscriptions: Subscriptions total {{symbol}}{{amount}}/mo. Audit these — unused ones are silent money drains.
diagnostic.alertFixedHigh: Fixed costs are {{pct}}% of income. Very little flexibility.

rewards.title: Rewards
rewards.gamificationHelper: Variable reward schedules — not guaranteed rewards — keep dopamine engagement high over time. XP and streaks here follow that model. Your data stays private and local.
rewards.skipTokensHelper: Life happens. A missed day breaks streaks and — research shows — triggers full abandonment for 1 in 3 users. Skip tokens exist to protect your momentum, not excuse laziness. Use them.

rewardsPlaceholder: Points and streaks will appear here. Gamification is optional.

social.title: Social
social.description: Plan social moments with gentle prompts and reminders.
social.templatesHelper: Choose a template to prefill a plan.
social.reminderHelperTtsOn: Reminders can include sound while text-to-speech is on.
social.reminderHelperTtsOff: Reminders stay silent while text-to-speech is off.
social.stepsHelper: Optional: add small steps to reduce friction.
social.aiPrivacy: If enabled, we only send the template summary and energy level. No personal data.
social.aiFallbackUsed: AI unavailable. Using the offline template instead.
social.aiQuotaExhausted: You have used your free AI suggestion. Sign in or upgrade to unlock more.
social.reflectionHelper: A quick check-in helps you understand your social energy patterns.

checkIn.label: Quick check-in
checkIn.description: Send a short, friendly hello without pressure.
checkIn.title: Check in with someone
checkIn.notes: Keep it light—one sentence is enough.

steps.first: Pick one person to reach out to.
steps.second: Write a simple message like “Hey, thinking of you.”

birthday.label: Birthday reminder
birthday.description: Send a warm message or small plan for a birthday.
birthday.title: Send a birthday message

steps.second: Add a memory or emoji if you want.

meetup.label: Low-key meetup
meetup.description: Invite someone to a simple, low-pressure hangout.
meetup.title: Plan a gentle meetup
meetup.notes: Offer two options so it’s easy to say yes or no.

steps.first: Choose a calm place or activity.
steps.second: Suggest a time window instead of a fixed slot.
steps.third: Send the invite and wait for a reply.

support.label: Supportive call
support.description: Prepare for a caring call or voice note.
support.title: Offer support
support.notes: You can keep it brief and gentle.

steps.first: Note one thing you want to say.
steps.second: Ask if they have time for a quick call.
steps.third: Follow up with a supportive message.

gallery.empty: No templates for this filter. Try a different depth level.

friendMemeShare.title: Share something funny
friendMemeShare.desc: Send a meme or video that instantly made you think of them.
friendMemeShare.notes: Low-stakes and warm. No reply needed.
friendMemeShare.step1: Find something that reminded you of them
friendMemeShare.starter1: This made me immediately think of you 😄
friendMemeShare.starter2: Okay I had to send you this, it's too relevant to us
friendMemeShare.starter3: No context needed, just thought you'd appreciate this 😂

friendVoiceNote.title: Send a voice note
friendVoiceNote.desc: Record a quick hello — warmer than text, easier than a call.
friendVoiceNote.notes: Keep it short and genuine. '30 seconds, no script' is enough.
friendVoiceNote.starter1: Hey! Sending a quick voice note because I was thinking about you. Hope you're doing well ✨
friendVoiceNote.starter2: Just a 30-second hello — no need to reply, just wanted you to know I'm thinking of you 🤍

friendCoffee.title: Coffee catch-up
friendCoffee.desc: A low-key in-person hang with no agenda — just time together.
friendCoffee.notes: Offer two time slots to make it easy to say yes.
friendCoffee.step2: Pick a time and place that works for both
friendCoffee.starter1: Hey, I've been meaning to properly catch up. Coffee or tea sometime this week or next?
friendCoffee.starter2: Miss your face! Want to grab coffee with zero agenda — just catch up?
friendCoffee.starter3: Are you free for a low-key coffee? I have maybe an hour and I want to hear what's going on with you.

friendRealCheckin.title: Real check-in
friendRealCheckin.desc: Ask how they're actually doing — not the surface answer.
friendRealCheckin.notes: This one takes a little courage. It's worth it.
friendRealCheckin.step1: Think about how they've seemed lately
friendRealCheckin.step2: Send a direct question that opens the door
friendRealCheckin.step3: Listen without rushing to fix anything
friendRealCheckin.starter1: Hey, I want to ask properly — how are you actually doing lately? Not the surface answer 💙
friendRealCheckin.starter2: I realized I haven't asked you how you're *really* doing in a while. What's going on with you?
friendRealCheckin.starter3: Checking in on you properly. You've seemed [tired/busy/quiet] lately and I wanted to make space if you need it.

celebrateWin.title: Celebrate a win
celebrateWin.desc: Acknowledge something specific they accomplished — it matters.
celebrateWin.notes: Be specific. Generic praise lands differently than 'I noticed what you did.'
celebrateWin.step1: Name the specific thing you're celebrating
celebrateWin.starter1: I just heard about [thing] and I needed to say — that is a huge deal and I'm so proud of you 🎉
celebrateWin.starter2: Can we take a moment to celebrate you?? [specific achievement] is amazing!
celebrateWin.starter3: I don't think you're giving yourself enough credit for [thing]. That was genuinely impressive.

gratitude.title: Express gratitude
gratitude.desc: Tell them what you appreciate about them — specifically.
gratitude.notes: Specificity is everything. Mention the exact thing.
gratitude.step1: Think of one thing they did that meant something
gratitude.step2: Write it out — the more specific, the more it lands
gratitude.starter1: I was thinking about you and I just want to say thank you for [specific thing]. It meant more than I said at the time.
gratitude.starter2: Random appreciation post: you are [specific quality] and I'm really grateful you're in my life.
gratitude.starter3: I don't say this enough, but [specific thing you did] genuinely helped me and I want you to know that.

friendReconnect.title: Reconnect
friendReconnect.desc: Reach out after a gap — low pressure, just saying hi.
friendReconnect.notes: No need to explain the gap. Just show up.
friendReconnect.step1: Write a short, warm message — no lengthy explanation
friendReconnect.step2: Ask one open question so the conversation can flow
friendReconnect.step3: Hit send and leave it open
friendReconnect.starter1: Hey stranger! Life has been a lot lately. I miss you and wanted to reach out — how have you been?
friendReconnect.starter2: I've been thinking about you. I know it's been a while — no pressure at all, just wanted to say hi.
friendReconnect.starter3: Okay I know it's been ages, but I saw [something] and thought of you. How's life?

friend36q.title: 36 Questions
friend36q.desc: Arthur Aron's closeness-building conversation — tried and tested.
friend36q.notes: Do the questions in order. Both answer each one.
friend36q.step1: Choose a comfortable setting with at least 90 minutes
friend36q.step3: Move to Set 2 (13–24) when you're both ready
friend36q.step4: Finish with Set 3 (25–36) and the 4-minute eye contact
friend36q.starter1: I came across this thing called the '36 questions that build closeness' and it looked really interesting. Want to try it together?
friend36q.starter2: I want to do something different when we hang out — there's this research-backed conversation format I'm curious about. Game?

friendSupport.title: Be there for them
friendSupport.desc: Reach out to someone going through a hard time.
friendSupport.notes: Ask what they need instead of assuming. Being present is enough.
friendSupport.step1: Check in gently — 'I've been thinking about you'
friendSupport.step2: Ask what would help: vent, problem-solve, or just not be alone?
friendSupport.step3: Follow their lead — don't try to fix, just listen
friendSupport.step4: Follow up in a few days
friendSupport.starter1: Hey, I've noticed you seem to be going through a hard time. I'm here if you want to talk — no pressure, but I'm available.
friendSupport.starter2: I don't need you to be okay. I just want to be with you in whatever you're going through. Call when you want?
friendSupport.starter3: I'm making space for you. What do you need right now — to vent, to problem-solve, or just to not be alone?

romanticAppreciation.title: Appreciation moment
romanticAppreciation.desc: Tell them something specific you appreciate — real, not generic.
romanticAppreciation.notes: Notice one thing they did or are this week. Say that.
romanticAppreciation.step1: Think of something specific they did or said recently
romanticAppreciation.step2: Tell them — in person, text, or a quick note
romanticAppreciation.starter1: I just want to say — I notice [specific thing you do] and I appreciate it more than I show.
romanticAppreciation.starter2: Random mid-day appreciation: you are [specific quality] and I'm lucky.
romanticAppreciation.starter3: I was thinking about [specific memory or thing] and it made me smile. Thank you for being you.

romanticDateIdea.title: Propose a date idea
romanticDateIdea.desc: Suggest something simple and intentional — no elaborate planning needed.
romanticDateIdea.notes: Simple works. The intention is what matters.
romanticDateIdea.step1: Think of one low-key idea you'd both enjoy
romanticDateIdea.step2: Propose it warmly — 'What if we did X?'
romanticDateIdea.step3: Confirm the details once they're in
romanticDateIdea.starter1: Random idea: [simple date idea]. Want to? We can keep it super low-key.
romanticDateIdea.starter2: I want to spend some intentional time with you. What if we did [idea] this weekend?
romanticDateIdea.starter3: What if we had a no-phones night this week and just [activity]?

romanticQuality.title: Intentional time together
romanticQuality.desc: Plan time that's truly just about being together — no errands, no screens.
romanticQuality.notes: Protect the time. Turn off notifications.
romanticQuality.step1: Pick a block of time and protect it
romanticQuality.starter1: I want to plan something that's just about us — no errands, no screens, just time together.

romanticCheckin.title: Relationship check-in
romanticCheckin.desc: A calm conversation about how you're both feeling as a couple.
romanticCheckin.notes: Not about problems — about staying connected. Use 'I' statements.
romanticCheckin.step1: Agree on a calm time with no time pressure
romanticCheckin.step2: Each share one thing that's been good and one that needs attention
romanticCheckin.step3: Talk about what you each need more of
romanticCheckin.step4: Close with an appreciation for each other
romanticCheckin.starter1: I want to have one of those 'how are we doing?' conversations — no urgency or conflict, just checking in together.
romanticCheckin.starter2: Can we do a relationship check-in soon? I want to make sure we're both feeling good and connected.

romanticDreams.title: Dream conversation
romanticDreams.desc: Talk about what you're each hoping for in life — not logistics, just dreams.
romanticDreams.notes: Stay curious, not evaluative. This is about understanding, not deciding.
romanticDreams.step2: Each share a personal dream — something you rarely say out loud
romanticDreams.step3: Ask follow-up questions and listen deeply
romanticDreams.starter1: I want to talk about our dreams sometime — not logistics, just what we're each hoping for in life. Up for it?

familyUpdate.title: Quick family update
familyUpdate.desc: Share a small life update and ask how they're doing.
familyUpdate.notes: Even a short message keeps the relationship warm.
familyUpdate.step1: Think of one thing from your week worth sharing
familyUpdate.step2: Send it with a simple question about them
familyUpdate.starter1: Hey! Quick update from my end: [one thing that happened]. How are you doing?
familyUpdate.starter2: Thinking of you. Here's a photo from [thing]. Miss you!
familyUpdate.starter3: Just wanted to check in. How's everything going on your end?

familyPhoto.title: Share a memory
familyPhoto.desc: Send an old photo or memory that made you smile — and think of them.
familyPhoto.notes: Simple, warm, and it takes two minutes.
familyPhoto.step1: Find a photo or memory that involves them
familyPhoto.step2: Send it with a short note about what it means to you
familyPhoto.starter1: Found this old photo of us! 😄 Remember [thing]? This made my day.
familyPhoto.starter2: Sharing a memory that made me smile today — and made me think of you.

familyCall.title: Family catch-up call
familyCall.desc: Schedule a real phone or video call — no agenda, just connection.
familyCall.notes: Offer a few time slots to reduce friction.
familyCall.step1: Reach out to check their availability
familyCall.step2: Set a specific time so it actually happens
familyCall.step3: Call and let the conversation flow naturally
familyCall.starter1: Hey, are you free for a call sometime this week? No agenda, I just want to catch up properly.
familyCall.starter2: Miss your voice. Can we schedule a call? Even 20 minutes would be great.

familyHelp.title: Offer concrete help
familyHelp.desc: Make a specific offer — not 'let me know if you need anything.'
familyHelp.notes: Specific offers are easier to accept than open-ended ones.
familyHelp.step1: Think of one concrete thing you can genuinely offer
familyHelp.step2: Reach out with the offer — make it easy to say yes
familyHelp.starter1: I want to help with [specific thing]. Can I [specific offer]? No need to have it all figured out.
familyHelp.starter2: I know you have a lot on right now. I'd like to [specific thing] if that would help at all.

acquaintanceCongrats.title: Send congratulations
acquaintanceCongrats.desc: Acknowledge a milestone or achievement they shared.
acquaintanceCongrats.step1: Note the specific thing you're congratulating them on
acquaintanceCongrats.starter1: Congratulations on [milestone]! That's a big deal and you should be proud.
acquaintanceCongrats.starter2: I saw your news about [thing] — that's really exciting. Congratulations!

acquaintanceReconnect.title: Light reconnect
acquaintanceReconnect.desc: A low-key message after some time has passed.
acquaintanceReconnect.notes: No explanation needed for the gap.
acquaintanceReconnect.step2: Ask one simple question to invite a reply
acquaintanceReconnect.starter1: Hey! It's been a while. I was thinking about [shared thing] and thought I'd reach out. How have you been?
acquaintanceReconnect.starter2: Hi! Random but I was reminded of you recently. Hope you're doing well — how are things?

acquaintanceCoffee.title: Suggest coffee
acquaintanceCoffee.desc: Propose a low-pressure meetup to get to know them better.
acquaintanceCoffee.notes: Keep it easy to accept. 'No agenda, just chat' removes pressure.
acquaintanceCoffee.step1: Reach out and suggest a simple meetup
acquaintanceCoffee.step2: Agree on a time and place
acquaintanceCoffee.starter1: I'd love to actually catch up properly sometime — would you be up for a coffee? Totally low-key.

coworkerRecognition.title: Recognize their work
coworkerRecognition.desc: Call out something specific they did well.
coworkerRecognition.notes: Be specific about what they did and why it mattered.
coworkerRecognition.step1: Name the specific thing they did
coworkerRecognition.step2: Send a direct, genuine note of recognition
coworkerRecognition.starter1: I just wanted to say — the way you handled [specific thing] was genuinely impressive. Nice work.
coworkerRecognition.starter2: I don't think you got enough credit for [thing]. I noticed and wanted to say something.

coworkerCasual.title: Non-work check-in
coworkerCasual.desc: Ask how they're doing beyond the task list.
coworkerCasual.notes: A simple human question builds real rapport over time.
coworkerCasual.step1: Find a natural moment — break, lunch, or a DM
coworkerCasual.starter1: Non-work question: how was your weekend / how are you doing beyond the tasks?
coworkerCasual.starter2: Hey, stepping outside work stuff for a sec — how are things with you generally?

coworkerLunch.title: Lunch or coffee
coworkerLunch.desc: Propose a casual lunch or coffee with no work agenda.
coworkerLunch.notes: The 'no work agenda' part makes a real difference.
coworkerLunch.step1: Reach out and propose a day
coworkerLunch.step2: Find a time that works for both of you
coworkerLunch.starter1: Want to grab lunch or coffee sometime this week? No work agenda — just actually talk.

coworkerFeedback.title: Ask for feedback
coworkerFeedback.desc: Request honest, growth-focused feedback from someone you trust.
coworkerFeedback.notes: Be specific about the area. Be ready to listen without defending.
coworkerFeedback.step1: Decide what area you want feedback on
coworkerFeedback.step3: Listen fully, say thank you, take notes
coworkerFeedback.starter1: I'm trying to grow and I really respect your perspective. Would you be open to giving me some honest feedback on [area]?

bossUpdate.title: Proactive update
bossUpdate.desc: Share a brief status update before you're asked.
bossUpdate.notes: Proactive communication builds trust and reduces anxiety on both sides.
bossUpdate.step1: Summarize where things stand in 2–3 sentences
bossUpdate.step2: Note any blockers or things you need
bossUpdate.step3: Send it — clear is better than perfect
bossUpdate.starter1: Quick update on [project]: [status]. Anything I should prioritize differently?
bossUpdate.starter2: Wanted to proactively share where things stand with [thing] before our next sync.

boss1on1.title: 1:1 preparation
boss1on1.desc: Prepare for your regular 1:1 so you use the time well.
boss1on1.notes: Come with questions and topics. It signals you value the time.
boss1on1.step1: Review open items or blockers you want to raise
boss1on1.step2: Note one thing you want feedback on
boss1on1.step3: Write one question about growth or direction
boss1on1.step4: Go in with your list — be the one who drives the agenda

bossGrowth.title: Growth conversation
bossGrowth.desc: Request a conversation focused on your development and direction.
bossGrowth.notes: Come with a specific area or question. Make it easy to help you.
bossGrowth.step1: Decide what you want to grow in or understand
bossGrowth.step2: Request a short meeting — '20 minutes about my development'
bossGrowth.step3: In the meeting: share your goal, ask one clear question
bossGrowth.starter1: I'd love to schedule a quick conversation about my growth path — would you have 20 minutes sometime soon?

neighborHello.title: Say hello
neighborHello.desc: A simple, warm hello to your neighbor.
neighborHello.notes: Neighboring is underrated. A short hello goes a long way.
neighborHello.step1: Wave, knock, or send a short note
neighborHello.starter1: Hey neighbor! Just wanted to say hi and check you're doing well.
neighborHello.starter2: Hi! I have [extra produce/item] if you'd want it — also just wanted to say hello.

neighborHangout.title: Neighbor hangout
neighborHangout.desc: Suggest getting together for a drink or coffee.
neighborHangout.notes: Low-key and local makes it easy.
neighborHangout.step1: Make the suggestion — casual and open
neighborHangout.starter1: Would you ever want to grab a drink or coffee sometime? I'd love to actually get to know my neighbors.

onlineGaming.title: Gaming session
onlineGaming.desc: Plan a proper online session — not just 'sometime'.
onlineGaming.notes: Scheduling it makes it real. Set a time and stick to it.
onlineGaming.step1: Agree on a game and day
onlineGaming.step2: Set a specific time and hop on
onlineGaming.starter1: Want to play [game] sometime this week? Been a while since we've had a session.
onlineGaming.starter2: Miss our [game/hangout] sessions. Are you free this week to do a proper one?

onlineVideo.title: Video catch-up
onlineVideo.desc: Actually see each other face to face — even online.
onlineVideo.notes: Video calls feel closer than text. Worth scheduling.
onlineVideo.step2: Agree and put it in the calendar
onlineVideo.step3: Show up and just hang out
onlineVideo.starter1: Want to do a video call soon? I miss actually seeing your face.
onlineVideo.starter2: Are you up for a proper video hang? We can just chill and talk.

pomodoroSection: Pomodoro timers and checklist progress bars will appear here.
languageToggleHelper: Switch languages without leaving onboarding. The toggle stays in the same predictable spot.
ttsToggleHelper: Turn text-to-speech reminders on or off from accessibility preferences.
calendarPlaceholder: The calendar will display colour‑coded events with TTS reminders.
calendarIntro: Plan your day or week with soft colors and gentle reminders.
calendarConfirmDelete: Delete event {{title}}? This will not delete the linked task.
settingsDescription: Personalise accessibility preferences and control gamification.
settingsFontOption: Choose between standard and dyslexia‑friendly fonts.
settingsGamificationOption: Show or hide points and streaks.
settingsThemeHelper: Choose light, dark, or high-contrast to reduce visual strain.
settingsFontHelper: Switch to dyslexia-friendly fonts or larger text for steadier reading.
settingsMotionHelper: Reduce motion to lower sensory load; we only animate essentials.
settingsTtsHelper: Keep audible cues on if you prefer reminders read aloud.

onboarding.intro: Complete this quick onboarding to unlock Tasks, Calendar, Budget, Rewards, and Settings.

flow.autosave: Your progress saves after each change so you can pause and return.
flow.resume: We reopen the exact step you left on to keep the sequence predictable.
flow.validationReminder: Finish the current step to enable Next.

predictableSteps.accessibility: Step 1 of 4: Choose text, contrast, motion, and TTS before moving on.
predictableSteps.neurodivergence: Step 2 of 4: Pick a preset and supports to seed recommendations.
predictableSteps.quickSetup: Step 3 of 4: Enter one goal and a starter budget to populate dashboards.
predictableSteps.tour: Step 4 of 4: Confirm navigation and reminder preferences so you know where to return.

languageToggle.aria: Toggle app language. Button shows the destination language: {{language}}.
languageToggle.helper: Keep the language toggle visible for predictable navigation.

ttsToggle.aria: Toggle text-to-speech reminders for accessible alerts.
ttsToggle.helper: Spoken reminders can be switched on or off at any time.

accessibility.title: Accessibility preferences
accessibility.subtitle: Select the reading and sensory options that keep you comfortable.
accessibility.helper: We apply these readability and sensory settings everywhere for a consistent, predictable layout.
accessibility.ttsHelper: Keep audible cues on if you prefer reminders read aloud.

neurodivergence.title: Neurodivergence presets
neurodivergence.subtitle: Choose a preset to pre-load supportive defaults.
neurodivergence.helper: Pick one starting point; you can adjust supports later without losing predictability.

adhd.title: ADHD
adhd.desc: Chunk tasks, focus timers, gentle alerts.

asd.title: Autistic
asd.desc: Predictable layouts, sensory calm, routine helpers.

dyslexia.title: Dyslexia
dyslexia.desc: Readable fonts, pacing cues, simplified steps.

auditory.title: Auditory processing
auditory.desc: Visual cues, captions, and slower speech rate.

quickSetup.title: Quick goals and budget
quickSetup.subtitle: Capture one goal and a starter budget to populate your dashboards.
quickSetup.helper: We only ask for one goal and a simple budget so you can finish quickly and keep steps predictable.
quickSetup.goalLabel: What should we help you achieve first?
quickSetup.goalPlaceholder: Example: Finish the midterm project before Friday
quickSetup.goalHint: We will create a starter task and reminders based on this goal.
quickSetup.budgetHint: Use a round number—we will help you refine categories later.
quickSetup.preview: Preview of what will be created:

tour.title: Mini tour
tour.subtitle: Confirm you know where things live and if you want gentle reminders.
tour.helper: This final step confirms navigation so the app always opens to predictable dashboards.
tour.summary: You will see dashboards for Tasks, Calendar, Budget, and Rewards once you finish this step.

items.tasks: Tasks: break projects into friendly steps.
items.budget: Budget: track income, spending, and payoff progress.

tour.reminders: Send a brief reminder to return if I pause
tour.acknowledge: I understand how to open these sections

sync.reviewHelper: Review changes that could not sync yet and choose which version to keep.

calendarSuggestedFocusHelper: We auto-place up to three unscheduled tasks into open time blocks for this day.
calendarSuggestedFocusEmpty: No open window fits your unscheduled tasks right now.

routines.title: Routines
routines.description: Habits compound. A 30-minute morning routine done consistently for 90 days creates more change than a perfect plan done once.
routines.emptyBody: A single consistent routine — even 10 minutes — is more valuable than any app, book, or plan. Start with one thing you want to do every day. Build from there.
routines.anchorHelper: Habit stacking: link a new behavior to one you already do automatically. BJ Fogg's research shows this doubles implementation success versus starting cold.
routines.anchorDisplay: After {{habit}}, I will do this routine

hubermanMorning.title: Huberman Morning Protocol
hubermanMorning.why: Morning light exposure sets your circadian clock, cortisol peak drives alertness, and delaying caffeine prevents afternoon crashes.
hubermanMorning.step2: Go outside within 30–60 min — face the morning sky for 10–15 min
hubermanMorning.step4: Do 10 min of light movement or stretching
hubermanMorning.step5: Cold shower or face splash (30 sec) — activates alertness

morningPages.title: Morning Pages
morningPages.why: Longhand writing before screens clears mental noise, surfaces subconscious concerns, and trains creative thinking over time.
morningPages.step1: Sit with pen and paper before screens
morningPages.step2: Write 3 pages longhand — stream of consciousness, no editing
morningPages.step3: Do not reread until at least a week later

deepWorkLaunch.title: Deep Work Launch
deepWorkLaunch.why: Protecting your peak cognitive hours for deep work — before reactive communication — compounds output over weeks.
deepWorkLaunch.step1: Write today's single most important task (MIT)
deepWorkLaunch.step2: Clear your desk of everything not related to that task
deepWorkLaunch.step3: Set a 90-min focus timer before checking messages
deepWorkLaunch.step4: Put phone in another room or drawer

exerciseMorning.title: Morning Exercise
exerciseMorning.why: Morning exercise boosts dopamine, serotonin, and BDNF for the next 4–6 hours. Doing it first removes the decision entirely.
exerciseMorning.step1: Put on workout clothes before any decision-making
exerciseMorning.step2: Do 20–30 min of cardio or resistance training
exerciseMorning.step3: Do NOT check phone until workout is complete
exerciseMorning.step4: Take protein or meal within 60 min of finishing

hubermanEvening.title: Huberman Evening Protocol
hubermanEvening.why: Bright light after sunset delays melatonin by up to 3 hours. Dimming lights early accelerates sleep onset and improves deep-sleep quality.
hubermanEvening.step1: View sunset or dim lights 2+ hours before bed
hubermanEvening.step2: No bright overhead lights after 9pm — use lamps
hubermanEvening.step3: No caffeine after 2pm (if you haven't already)
hubermanEvening.step4: Do not eat within 2–3 hours of sleep

powerDownHour.title: Power-Down Hour
powerDownHour.why: A structured wind-down signals the nervous system to shift from sympathetic to parasympathetic — making sleep faster and deeper.
powerDownHour.step1: First 20 min: finish small tasks and tomorrow's to-do list
powerDownHour.step2: Next 20 min: hygiene (shower, brush teeth, skincare)
powerDownHour.step3: Final 20 min: only relaxing activities — reading, light stretching, breathing

eveningReview.title: Evening Review
eveningReview.why: Stoic journaling lowers rumination by externalizing thoughts, and gratitude practice measurably improves mood and sleep quality.
eveningReview.step1: Write: What did I do well today?
eveningReview.step2: Write: What could I have done better?
eveningReview.step3: Write: What am I grateful for today?
eveningReview.step4: Close your notebook — do not review screens after this

tomorrowPrep.title: Tomorrow Prep
tomorrowPrep.why: Pre-deciding tomorrow removes decision fatigue at the start of the next day and closes the Zeigarnik loop on open tasks.
tomorrowPrep.step2: Set out anything you'll need (gym bag, work items, etc)
tomorrowPrep.step4: Close all browser tabs and apps

weeklyReview.title: Weekly Review
weeklyReview.why: Weekly reviews catch drift before it compounds. Reviewing what blocked you turns repeated failures into system improvements.
weeklyReview.step2: Review last week: what got done, what didn't, what blocked you
weeklyReview.step3: Set 3 intentions for the coming week
weeklyReview.step4: Schedule any important tasks as calendar blocks
weeklyReview.step5: Review your finances — check spending vs budget

healthAudit.title: Weekly Health Audit
healthAudit.why: What gets measured gets managed. A 15-min weekly audit creates accountability without adding shame or complexity.
healthAudit.step1: Note sleep quality this week (1–5 scale)
healthAudit.step3: Note how many days you ate vegetables
healthAudit.step4: Identify the #1 health habit to improve next week

pomodoroBlock.title: Pomodoro Focus Block
pomodoroBlock.why: Fixed-interval focus with scheduled breaks matches natural ultradian rhythms and prevents the mental fatigue of open-ended work sessions.
pomodoroBlock.step1: Choose one task and write it down
pomodoroBlock.step2: Set 25-min timer — work on nothing else
pomodoroBlock.step4: Repeat 4 times then take a 20–30 min break

noPhoneMorning.title: No-Phone Morning
noPhoneMorning.why: Checking your phone first thing puts you in reactive mode. One hour of phone-free time builds internal focus before external demands hit.
noPhoneMorning.step1: Do not check your phone for the first 60 minutes after waking
noPhoneMorning.step2: Do your morning routine first (light, movement, breakfast)
noPhoneMorning.step3: After 60 min, check messages for a defined 10-min window only

zone2Cardio.title: Zone 2 Cardio
zone2Cardio.why: Zone 2 cardio is the single highest-ROI exercise for metabolic health, mitochondrial density, and longevity. 3–4 sessions/week is the minimum effective dose.
zone2Cardio.step1: Do 30–45 minutes of cardio at a 'conversational' pace — you could speak in sentences
zone2Cardio.step2: Keep heart rate around 60–70% of your max (roughly 180 minus your age)
zone2Cardio.step3: Aim for 3–4 sessions per week — this is the minimum effective dose for longevity

mobility.title: Daily Mobility Routine
mobility.why: Ten minutes of daily mobility prevents the chronic tightness that leads to injury. Done consistently, it reverses years of desk posture.
mobility.step1: Neck rolls: 5 slow circles each direction
mobility.step5: Arm across chest: 30 seconds each side

gallery.heading: Routine Template Library
gallery.subtitle: Research-backed routines. Pick one to add it to your schedule.
gallery.empty: No templates in this category yet.

streak.headline: {{days}}-Day Streak
streak.sub: Consistency is the compound interest of effort. Keep going.

allTasksDone.headline: All Done Today
allTasksDone.sub: You showed up. That's the whole game.

routineComplete.headline: Routine Complete
routineComplete.sub: You just won your morning. That compounds.

budgetGoal.headline: Budget Goal Hit
budgetGoal.sub: One category owned. Every number you face, you own.

firstTask.headline: First Task Created
firstTask.sub: Your brain can relax now. It's in the system.

firstRoutine.headline: First Routine Built
firstRoutine.sub: The backbone of your morning just got stronger.

levelUp.headline: Level {{level}} Reached
levelUp.sub: Growth is not linear — but you are moving forward.

welcomeBack.title: Welcome back — you were away for {{days}}.
welcomeBack.message1: No judgment here — just where you left off.
welcomeBack.message2: The hardest part is coming back. You just did it.
welcomeBack.continueCta: Pick up where I left off

upgrade.title: {{feature}} is a premium feature
upgrade.subtitle: Start your 10-day free trial — no credit card required.

pricing.heading: Simple, honest pricing
pricing.subheading: Start free. Upgrade when you're ready.
pricing.noCard: No credit card required for trial
pricing.trialConfirmed: 🎉 Trial activated! Billing portal coming soon.
pricing.trialConfirmedSub: Your account has been noted. We'll email you when billing is ready.
pricing.faq: Cancel anytime. Downgrade to free at any point — your data stays safe.

settingsPanel.aboutDescription: GYLIO is a neurodivergent-friendly productivity app built with accessibility, low cognitive load, and gentle UX at its core.
settingsPanel.tourDescription: Take the interactive tour again to rediscover features or show the app to someone new.
settingsPanel.timerInsight: Research shows the brain works in ~90-minute ultradian cycles. Shorter sprints (25 min) suit most tasks; longer sprints (45–90 min) require proportionally longer breaks to avoid cognitive fatigue. Find your window.
settingsPanel.timerBreakInsight: Longer focus sprints need longer breaks: 45 min → 15 min break; 90 min → 20 min break. Skipping breaks compounds mental fatigue.

welcome.title: Welcome to GYLIO
welcome.content: This quick tour walks you through the key features — it takes about 2 minutes. You can pause anytime and come back right where you left off.

navBar.title: Your main navigation
navBar.content: Switch between Tasks, Calendar, Budget, and more using this bar. Features marked with ✦ are available on the Premium plan.

taskInput.title: Add a task
taskInput.content: Type your task here. Be specific — 'Reply to Maria's email by 3 pm' beats 'check email'. Press N anytime to jump straight here.

taskEnergy.title: Match tasks to your energy
taskEnergy.content: Choose how much energy a task needs. Low battery? Pick Tiny or Low tasks. Feeling sharp? Tackle a High-energy one. This helps GYLIO suggest the right task at the right time.

taskSteps.title: Break big tasks down
taskSteps.content: Large tasks feel overwhelming. Tap 'Break into steps' to split one task into micro-steps — even 3 small steps make starting much easier.

pomodoro.title: Focus blocks (Pomodoro)
pomodoro.content: Work in short, timed sprints — 25 minutes on, then a break. Your brain's natural rhythm matches this cycle, so you stay in flow longer and feel less drained.

calendar.title: Your calendar
calendar.content: View your day, week, or month at a glance. Add events and see how your schedule looks so you can plan tasks around your real commitments.

budget.title: Budget tracker
budget.content: Track income and spending by category. The progress bars show exactly how much of each budget you've used this month — no surprises.

done.title: You're all set!
done.content: You know the essentials. Explore at your own pace — and restart this guide anytime from Settings or the Guide button in the header.
